skill.name: database-cloud-optimization-cost-optimize
skill.description: Analyze cloud spending and optimization opportunities across AWS, Azure, and GCP. Plan rightsizing, storage policies, budget controls, and operational safeguards.
skill.summary: Plan cloud cost reductions with rightsizing, storage, budget, and monitoring guidance.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command, C2 keyword, weak crypto, and sensitive-file alerts are false positives from Markdown fences, cloud API names, Terraform variable names, and words such as description. The skill is not malicious, but its playbook contains examples that can modify cloud infrastructure when copied and run with real credentials, so publication should include an operational-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: Cloud Infrastructure Mutation Examples
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for operational risk. The playbook includes AWS examples that stop EC2 instances and modify instance types. The default dry_run flag reduces risk, but copied code could affect availability when dry_run is disabled.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly calls EC2 stop and modify APIs in an automation example. It is legitimate FinOps guidance, but it can change production infrastructure.

medium_findings.title: Automated Storage Lifecycle Expiration
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for data-retention risk. The S3 lifecycle example expires noncurrent object versions after 90 days. This can reduce cost, but it can also remove rollback or retention data if applied without policy review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code creates and applies a lifecycle rule with NoncurrentVersionExpiration. The risk depends on deployment context, so severity is medium.

medium_findings.title: Automated Lambda Cost Controls Can Reduce Capacity
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for availability risk. The serverless example reads a budget setting and reduces Lambda concurrency when costs exceed a threshold. This should require change control and service-owner approval before production use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code invokes Lambda concurrency controls and references removing provisioned concurrency. It is cost-focused, but it can degrade service capacity.

low_findings.title: External Command Alerts Are Markdown Fence False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged backticks are Markdown code fences and inline resource references, not Ruby shell execution. No evidence found of shell command execution in the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are Markdown delimiters or inline code text. They do not execute commands and no executable wrapper is present.

low_findings.title: Dashboard CDN URLs Are External Loads, Not Exfiltration
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for hardcoded network URLs with low severity. The URLs load Chart.js and D3 in generated dashboard HTML examples. No evidence found that cost data or credentials are sent to those domains.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded URLs are visible script tags for public JavaScript libraries. They still create a supply-chain dependency if used as written.

low_findings.title: Environment Access Is Limited To Budget Configuration
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for environment access with low severity. The example reads MONTHLY_BUDGET and does not read secrets, tokens, or cloud credentials. No evidence found of environment data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact environment key is MONTHLY_BUDGET with a numeric default. The surrounding code uses it as a threshold only.

low_findings.title: Sensitive File Alerts Are Terraform Variable False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines reference a Terraform key_name variable, subnet IDs, and a tag value. No private key, certificate material, or credential value is embedded in the file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is a Terraform spot fleet template. The text names variables and tags but does not contain secret material.

low_findings.title: C2 Keyword Alerts Are Cloud API False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged terms appear in normal cloud inventory, monitoring, service, and networking examples. No evidence found of command-and-control behavior, beaconing, or unauthorized remote control.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is AWS SDK resource discovery and cost optimization. The same lines use boto3 service clients and describe APIs for legitimate inventory.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Alerts Are String-Match False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines contain words such as describe, description, DESC ordering, and lifecycle settings. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the flagged lines shows ordinary English and AWS describe APIs, not cryptographic primitives.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Are Legitimate Asset Inventory
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines collect resource IDs, VPC IDs, AMI variables, and tag joins for cloud cost analysis. No evidence found of hostile host reconnaissance or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are part of cost optimization workflows that require cloud resource inventory. This is expected behavior for the skill domain.

low_findings.title: Critical Heuristic Combination Is Not Supported By Context
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The heuristic combined Markdown code fences, public CDN script tags, and non-secret configuration access. No evidence found of obfuscation, credential theft, or malicious command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The underlying signals were reviewed in context and do not form a malicious chain. The remaining risk is operational change risk, not malware behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Production Change APIs In Documentation Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The playbook includes examples for stopping instances, changing instance types, applying S3 lifecycle policies, and changing Lambda concurrency. These are legitimate cost controls but require review, dry runs, backups, and rollback plans.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples use cloud provider APIs that can directly alter availability and retention. They are presented as cost optimization workflows, not stealthy behavior.

content.value_statement: Cloud bills often grow because teams lack clear cost visibility and safe optimization workflows. This skill helps analyze spend, plan savings, and apply controls with reliability safeguards.
actual_capabilities: Guides cloud spend analysis by service, resource, tag, and time period.
Recommends rightsizing opportunities for compute, databases, storage, containers, and serverless workloads.
Outlines reserved instance, Savings Plan, and spot instance evaluation workflows.
Provides monitoring, budget alert, anomaly detection, and dashboard patterns.
Includes rollback, staging validation, and approval guidance for cost-saving changes.
limitations: Requires billing, usage, and resource data to produce accurate recommendations.
Example automation must be reviewed before use in production accounts.
Savings estimates depend on current pricing, discounts, and workload behavior.
It does not replace cloud provider governance or service-owner approval.

use_cases.title: Reduce Monthly Cloud Spend
use_cases.description: Find waste, oversized resources, idle services, and quick wins across major cloud providers.

use_cases.title: Plan Safe Rightsizing Changes
use_cases.description: Prepare instance, database, storage, and serverless changes with risk notes and rollback steps.

use_cases.title: Improve Cost Governance
use_cases.description: Design tagging, budget alerts, anomaly detection, and recurring optimization reviews.

prompt_templates.title: Find Quick Savings
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this cloud bill summary and list the top quick savings opportunities. Include estimated impact, risk, and next actions.

prompt_templates.title: Rightsize Resources
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these utilization metrics and recommend rightsizing changes. Include before and after sizing, expected savings, and rollback steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design Cost Controls
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a cost control plan for this account structure. Include budgets, alerts, tagging rules, anomaly detection, and owner responsibilities.

prompt_templates.title: Build Optimization Roadmap
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a 90-day optimization roadmap for AWS, Azure, and GCP. Prioritize actions by savings, risk, dependency, and implementation effort.

output_examples.input: A monthly AWS bill shows high EC2, NAT Gateway, S3, and RDS costs.
output_examples.output: A prioritized savings plan with idle resource review, rightsizing candidates, storage lifecycle checks, NAT alternatives, and owner approval steps.

output_examples.input: A database cluster has low CPU use but strict availability requirements.
output_examples.output: A conservative rightsizing recommendation with maintenance windows, replica checks, benchmark validation, backup confirmation, and rollback criteria.

output_examples.input: Leadership wants monthly cloud cost governance across teams.
output_examples.output: A governance outline covering tags, budgets, anomaly alerts, review cadence, team accountability, and savings measurement.

best_practices: Validate recommendations with recent utilization, business context, and service-owner approval.
Use dry runs, staging tests, backups, and rollback plans before production changes.
Measure realized savings after implementation and keep optimization reviews recurring.
anti_patterns: Do not apply automated resizing or deletion rules without production impact review.
Do not treat low utilization alone as proof that a resource can be reduced.
Do not optimize for cost in ways that violate retention, compliance, or availability requirements.

faq.question: Does this skill connect to cloud accounts directly?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Access depends on the tools and credentials the user chooses to provide.

faq.question: Which cloud providers does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers AWS, Azure, and GCP concepts, with many examples focused on AWS services and APIs.

faq.question: Can it optimize database costs?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide rightsizing, storage review, and utilization analysis for database resources when metrics are available.

faq.question: Is the included automation production ready?
faq.answer: No. Treat automation examples as templates that need review, testing, permissions controls, and rollback planning.

faq.question: Can it estimate savings?
faq.answer: Yes, when pricing, usage, and utilization data are provided. Estimates should be verified against current provider pricing.

faq.question: What data should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide billing exports, resource lists, utilization metrics, tags, service ownership, and known availability or compliance constraints.
